meaning: Writing that expresses a strong opinion or perspective on a topic to persuade or argue.
Writing designed to convince the reader to agree with a particular viewpoint or take an action.
A reasoned case supporting a particular viewpoint, using evidence and logic.
Acknowledging and then refuting an opposing viewpoint to strengthen your own position.
An appeal to the writer's credibility or authority to persuade the audience.
An appeal to the reader's emotions to create sympathy or engagement.
An appeal to logic and reason, using facts and evidence to support a point.
Language specifically chosen to trigger an emotional response in the reader.
A commanding or directive verb used to instruct or urge the reader to act.
A short personal story used to illustrate a point and make writing relatable.
Numerical data used as evidence to support an argument and add credibility.
Quoting an authority or specialist to lend weight and credibility to an argument.
The level of formality in language, ranging from very formal to informal or colloquial.
Language that uses standard grammar and vocabulary, avoiding contractions and slang.
Informal language or expressions used in everyday speech.
A statement that declares something and ends with a full stop.
A sentence that expresses strong emotion and ends with an exclamation mark.
Acknowledging a valid point from the opposing side before countering it.
A tendency to favour one perspective over another, often one-sided or prejudiced.
The use of humour, irony or exaggeration to criticise or mock people and ideas.
